---
title: "Baltic Energy Transit Risk"
subtitle: "Exposure of post-2022 Baltic energy import infrastructure"
date: today
---

## The question

Before 2022, much of the Baltic region's gas arrived by pipeline from Russia.
After the invasion of Ukraine and the Nord Stream sabotage, that supply was cut
or abandoned, and imports moved onto a small set of coastal LNG terminals and
ports — Klaipėda, Świnoujście, Inkoo, and a handful of others.

That shift concentrated a lot of supply onto a few fixed points of geography.
This project asks how concentrated, and how exposed:

> After 2022, Baltic energy imports shifted onto a small set of coastal LNG
> terminals and ports. How spatially concentrated is that infrastructure, and
> how exposed is it — measured by capacity, by proximity to the Russian and
> Kaliningrad maritime boundary, and by dependence on the single Danish Straits
> chokepoint?

## How to read this site

- **Data** lists every source, all public, with citations and the cleaning steps.
- **Analysis** is the spatial work: the maps, the distance and buffer
  measurements, and the exposure index.

## Findings

The numbers behind these are on the [Analysis](02-analysis.qmd) page. The
terminal figures are compiled approximations (see [Data](01-data.qmd)), so read
the rankings as a structured view of exposure rather than precise measurement.

1. _Concentration:_ A handful of terminals — Świnoujście, Klaipėda, Inkoo —
   carry most of the region's regasification capacity. The Gulf of Finland and
   the southern Baltic each lean on one or two assets.
2. _Proximity:_ The terminals nearest Russian territory are the Gulf of Finland
   FSRUs and Klaipėda, which sits close to Kaliningrad. Proximity and capacity
   do not always coincide, which is what the index is meant to surface.
3. _Chokepoint dependence:_ Every cargo to these terminals transits the Danish
   Straits, so that single dependence is shared across the whole system — no
   terminal routes around it.

## What the exposure index is

The index combines three normalized components:

- proximity to the Russian/Kaliningrad maritime boundary,
- the asset's share of national supply capacity,
- reliance on passage through the Danish Straits.

The weights are stated on the Analysis page. The point of keeping it simple is
that every number can be explained out loud — there is no model doing work you
can't see.

## Limitations

This measures geographic exposure, not the probability of disruption. A terminal
sitting close to a maritime boundary is not a prediction that anything will
happen to it; it is a statement about geography.

A few specific caveats:

- The index uses proxy measures (distance, capacity share) rather than a causal
  model. Different reasonable weights would shift the rankings.
- It is a single-snapshot view. Capacity, routes, and terminals change.
- Maritime boundaries and "chokepoint reliance" are simplified. Real traffic
  routing is more complex than a single gate.
- Undersea cable and pipeline incidents in the region (2023–2025) are context,
  not modeled events.

The honest summary: this is a structured way to see where the exposure sits, not
a risk forecast.
